title: Best Tool-Use LLM: Compare Prices Pricing Grid
description: Picking an LLM with the strongest tool-use track record via TokenRate's Compare Prices grid — three picks with the per-tool-call cost tradeoffs.

sections.heading: Tool-Use / Function Calling: The Right Question Isn't "Which Model"
sections.body: TokenRate's new [Compare Prices grid](/tools/compare-prices) puts every model's per-token rates, context window, and quality score in a single side-by-side view. The point: stop flipping between provider pricing pages and OpenRouter tabs. You pick a provider dropdown, check the models you want, repeat for each provider, and the grid stacks every pick into one comparison table. For Tool-Use / Function Calling the picking decision usually defaults to "use the model my last project used" — which is roughly the worst possible heuristic in 2026, because the price-quality frontier has shifted three times in the past year. The right question is "which combination of price tier, context length, and quality score fits Tool-Use / Function Calling traffic patterns?" The [Compare Prices grid](/tools/compare-prices) is built for that question. This guide walks through three picks for Tool-Use / Function Calling and shows how to grid them. See also: [filter LLM models by tier, cost, quality](/blog/filter-llm-models-by-tier-cost-quality), [Value column vs tokens-per-dollar](/blog/value-column-vs-tokens-per-dollar-metric), and [how to pick an LLM by quality score and cost](/blog/how-to-pick-llm-by-quality-score-and-cost).

sections.heading: The Workload Profile of Tool-Use / Function Calling
sections.body: Tool-Use / Function Calling workloads have a few distinguishing characteristics: each call produces a tool selection + arguments; quality on selection matters more than fluency; tool-use benchmarks (BFCL, Tau-Bench) are the right quality proxy. That profile tells you which columns of the [Compare Prices grid](/tools/compare-prices) matter most. Quality (specifically tool-use benchmark scores), output cost (tool args are short but loops can run long), input cost (function schemas add tokens). It also tells you which tier you should be in. For most Tool-Use / Function Calling traffic, the [Balanced tier](/blog/flagship-balanced-fast-reasoning-llm-tiers) is the production default — quality high enough to ship to real users at scale, price low enough to make the unit economics work.

sections.heading: Top Pick: Claude Sonnet 4.7
sections.body: For Tool-Use / Function Calling, Claude Sonnet 4.7 is the default candidate. Pricing: $3.00 input / $15.00 output per 1M tokens. Context: 200K. Quality score: 80. Tier: balanced. Why it wins: top-tier tool-use accuracy at a production-default price point. Add it to the [Compare Prices grid](/tools/compare-prices) and the Value column makes the case visually (26.7 quality per dollar of input cost). Where it loses: on very high-volume tool-use loops where a smaller cheaper model can clear the bar.

sections.heading: Runner-Ups and When to Pick Them
sections.body: **GPT-5** ($1.25 / $10.00, Q82) — pick this when quality is non-negotiable and the bill is a rounding error against the value of correct output. **Gemini 2.5 Pro** ($1.25 / $10.00, Q78) — pick this when you want the production-default balance of quality (78) and price ($1.25 input). All three live in the same [Compare Prices view](/tools/compare-prices) so the comparison is one screen, not three browser tabs. For workload-specific cost modeling, run your token volume through [/tools/api-cost-estimator](/tools/api-cost-estimator).

sections.heading: Compare-Prices Workflow for Tool-Use / Function Calling
sections.body: Workflow: (1) open [/tools/compare-prices](/tools/compare-prices), (2) check the three picks across their provider dropdowns, (3) sort the resulting grid by Value column, (4) shortlist the top 1-2, (5) run an A/B against your real Tool-Use / Function Calling traffic for a week. The shortlisting step is where 90% of the time savings happen — the grid eliminates obvious losers (low quality, wrong context, output-cost surprises) in seconds. The grid pulls prices live from [OpenRouter](https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/models) and quality from a blended Arena AI + Artificial Analysis pipeline — both refresh on a 60-minute incremental cache, so the comparison reflects current rates not a baked-in snapshot. Run the comparison live at [/tools/compare-prices](/tools/compare-prices), then bookmark the URL for next month's price audit.

faq.question: How do I open the Compare Prices grid?
faq.answer: Two ways: click the 'Compare Prices' tab at the top of the calculator card on the home page, or navigate directly to /tools/compare-prices. The standalone page is also linked from the main navigation under 'Tools'.

faq.question: Can I share my comparison with teammates?
faq.answer: Yes — the page URL captures the current state. Send the link in Slack and your teammate sees the same grid. Useful for procurement and architecture-review meetings.

faq.question: Is the data live or cached?
faq.answer: Live from OpenRouter (prices) and a blended Arena AI + Artificial Analysis pipeline (quality), refreshed on a 60-minute incremental cache. So the grid is at most an hour stale.

faq.question: Where do I go after the grid to project monthly cost?
faq.answer: Once you've picked a winner, go to /tools/api-cost-estimator and plug in the model + your expected monthly token volume. The estimator does the per-1M math against your real workload mix.

ctaText: Run the comparison live at [/tools/compare-prices](/tools/compare-prices), then bookmark the URL for next month's price audit.
